"And if they turn out to be two, is not each of them one and different?"

"Certainly."

"And if each is one, and both are two, she will conceive the two as in a state of division, for if there were undivided they could only be conceived of as one?"

"True."

"The eye certainly did see both small and great, but only in a confused manner; they were not distinguished."

"Yes."

"Whereas the thinking mind, intending to light up the chaos, was compelled to reverse the process, and look at small and great as separate and not confused."

"Very true."

"Was not this the beginning of the enquiry 'What is great?' and 'What is small?'"

"Exactly so."

"And thus arose the distinction of the visible and the intelligible."

"Most true."

"This was what I meant when I spoke of impressions which invited the intellect, or the reverse -- those which are simultaneous with opposite impressions, invite thought; those which are not simultaneous do not."
refashioned:
"Exactly right," said Glaucon.

"So the soul must be perplexed when sense reports something as both hard and soft. And what about light and heavy -- when the same thing seems both?"

"Yes, these reports are puzzling and demand explanation."

"And in that perplexity," Socrates continued, "the soul naturally calls on calculation and intelligence. It needs to determine: are these one thing or two?"

"True."

"And if they turn out to be two, each is one thing, distinct from the other?"

"Certainly."

"If each is one and together they are two, the soul conceives them as divided -- because if they were undivided, they could only be conceived as one."

"True."

"Now, the eye did see both small and great, but only in a confused, blended way -- not as separate things."

"Yes."

"The thinking mind, trying to bring order to this chaos, was forced to reverse the process: to look at small and great as separate, not fused together."

"Exactly."

"And isn't this the origin of the inquiry 'What is greatness?' and 'What is smallness?'"

"Precisely."

"And so the distinction between the visible and the intelligible was born."

"Exactly right."

"This is what I meant earlier: impressions that simultaneously present opposites invite thought. Those that don't, don't."
elegant: When the senses report contradictions -- hard and soft, great and small in the same object -- the soul summons reason to separate what perception has confused. This forces the question 'What IS greatness?' as distinct from 'What IS smallness?' -- and thus the distinction between the visible and intelligible worlds is born.
summary: Sensory contradictions -- perceiving the same thing as both hard and soft, great and small -- force the soul to engage reason. The mind separates what the senses confuse, asking 'What IS greatness?' This is how the distinction between visible and intelligible reality arises. Impressions presenting simultaneous opposites are what provoke intellectual inquiry.
distilled: Philosophy begins when contradictory sense experience forces the mind to ask 'What IS this?' -- separating the intelligible from the visible.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in rapid dialectical exchange -- This passage gives Plato's genetic account of how philosophy begins: sensory contradiction forces abstraction -- 'What is great? What is small?' = the turn from particular sensible instances to universal intelligible Forms -- 'The distinction of the visible and the intelligible' directly maps onto the Divided Line from Book VI -- The argument that opposites provoke inquiry connects to the theory of Forms: we need Forms precisely because sensible things participate in opposite properties -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); Phaedo 74a-75d (argument from sensible equals to the Form of Equality); Parmenides 129a-130a

narrative:
"I understand," he said, "and agree with you."

"And to which class do unity and number belong?"

"I do not know," he replied.

"Think a little and you will see that what has preceded will supply the answer; for if simple unity could be adequately perceived by the sight or by any other sense, then, as we were saying in the case of the finger, there would be nothing to attract towards being; but when there is some contradiction always present, and one is the reverse of one and involves the conception of plurality, then thought begins to be aroused within us, and the soul perplexed and wanting to arrive at a decision asks 'What is absolute unity?' This is the way in which the study of the one has a power of drawing and converting the mind to the contemplation of true being."

"And surely," he said, "this occurs notably in the case of one; for we see the same thing to be both one and infinite in multitude?"

"Yes," I said; "and this being true of one must be equally true of all number?"

"Certainly."

"And all arithmetic and calculation have to do with number?"
refashioned:
"I understand and agree," said Glaucon.

"Now, to which class do unity and number belong -- the thought-provoking or the non-provoking?"

"I'm not sure."

"Think about it," said Socrates. "The answer follows from what we've just said. If simple unity could be adequately perceived by sight or any other sense, it would be like the finger -- nothing would attract the mind toward being. But there IS a contradiction always present: one is the reverse of one and implies plurality. This arouses thought. The perplexed soul, wanting to resolve the contradiction, asks: 'What is absolute unity?' This is how the study of the one draws and converts the mind to contemplate true being."

"That makes sense," said Glaucon. "We see the same thing as both one and infinitely many."

"Yes, and if this is true of one, it's equally true of all number."

"Certainly."

"And all arithmetic and calculation deal with number?"
elegant: Unity provokes thought because the same thing is simultaneously one and many. The perplexed soul asks 'What is absolute unity?' -- and this question draws the mind toward being. Since this is true of all number, arithmetic and calculation are inherently philosophical disciplines.
summary: Socrates argues that unity belongs to the thought-provoking class because the same thing appears as both one and many, creating a contradiction that forces the soul to ask 'What is absolute unity?' Since this applies to all number, arithmetic inherently draws the mind toward contemplation of true being.
distilled: The paradox of the one and the many -- that every thing is simultaneously one and infinite in multitude -- makes arithmetic a gateway to philosophical inquiry.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- The One and the Many problem is among the most fundamental in Greek philosophy, treated extensively in the Parmenides -- 'What is absolute unity?' = the Form of Unity, an intelligible reality grasped by reason alone -- Every sensible thing is both one (itself) and many (having parts, properties, relations) -- this contradiction provokes philosophical inquiry -- Cross-ref: Parmenides 128e-130a (the problem of the one and many); Philebus 14c-18d (the one and many as the greatest philosophical problem); Divided Line (mathematical reasoning as dianoia)

narrative:
"Yes."

"And they appear to lead the mind towards truth?"

"Yes, in a very remarkable manner."

"Then this is knowledge of the kind for which we are seeking, having a double use, military and philosophical; for the man of war must learn the art of number or he will not know how to array his troops, and the philosopher also, because he has to rise out of the sea of change and lay hold of true being, and therefore he must be an arithmetician."
refashioned:
"Yes," said Glaucon.

"And arithmetic appears to lead the mind toward truth?"

"Yes, remarkably so."

"Then arithmetic is exactly the kind of knowledge we've been looking for -- it serves a double purpose, military and philosophical. The warrior needs it to organize his troops. The philosopher needs it to rise out of the sea of change and grasp true being. Both must be arithmeticians."
elegant: Arithmetic serves both purposes: the warrior needs it to array troops, the philosopher needs it to rise from the flux of becoming and grasp true being.
summary: Socrates concludes that arithmetic perfectly meets their criteria: it is useful both militarily (for organizing troops) and philosophically (for rising above the world of change to grasp true being).
distilled: Arithmetic serves the double purpose of practical military organization and philosophical ascent from becoming to being.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Rise out of the sea of change' (genesis) = transcend the world of becoming to reach the world of being -- 'Double use, military and philosophical' satisfies the dual requirement established in 7.23 -- Arithmetic is the first subject formally added to the philosopher's curriculum -- The 'sea of change' metaphor echoes Heraclitean flux, which Plato believes can only be overcome through rational inquiry -- Cross-ref: the curriculum list will include arithmetic, geometry, solid geometry, astronomy, harmonics, dialectic; Cratylus 439b-440e on Heraclitean flux

narrative:
"And our guardian is both warrior and philosopher?"

"Certainly."

"Then this is a kind of knowledge which legislation may fitly prescribe; and we must endeavour to persuade those who are to be the principal men of our State to go and learn arithmetic, not as amateurs, but they must carry on the study until they see the nature of numbers with the mind only; nor again, like merchants or retail-traders, with a view to buying or selling, but for the sake of their military use, and of the soul herself; and because this will be the easiest way for her to pass from becoming to truth and being."

"That is excellent," he said.

"Yes," I said, "and now having spoken of it, I must add how charming the science is! and in how many ways it conduces to our desired end, if pursued in the spirit of a philosopher, and not of a shopkeeper!"
refashioned:
"And our guardian is both warrior and philosopher?"

"Certainly."

"Then the law should prescribe arithmetic for them. We must persuade the state's future leaders to study it -- not casually, but deeply, until they grasp the nature of numbers with the mind alone. Not like merchants calculating for profit, but for military use and for the sake of the soul itself -- because arithmetic is the easiest path from becoming to truth and being."

"Excellent," said Glaucon.

"And now that we've established this," Socrates added, "let me say how delightful this science is, and how powerfully it serves our purpose -- when pursued in the spirit of a philosopher, not a shopkeeper!"
elegant: The law should prescribe arithmetic studied deeply, for the mind alone -- not for commercial profit but for the soul's passage from becoming to being. Pursued philosophically rather than commercially, arithmetic is both charming and powerful.
summary: Socrates prescribes arithmetic as law for future rulers, insisting it be studied deeply and purely -- not for commercial gain like merchants, but to grasp numbers with the mind alone, enabling the soul's passage from becoming to being.
distilled: Arithmetic must be studied philosophically for the soul's ascent, not commercially for profit -- the spirit of inquiry determines whether knowledge liberates or remains mundane.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- The contrast between philosophical and commercial arithmetic is fundamental: the same subject can serve opposite purposes depending on the spirit of inquiry -- 'See the nature of numbers with the mind only' = pure mathematical reasoning, grasping intelligible number distinct from sensible counting -- 'Not like merchants or retail-traders' reflects the aristocratic Greek disdain for commercial pursuits (banausic arts) -- Cross-ref: the Divided Line places mathematical reasoning (dianoia) above opinion but below dialectical knowledge (noesis) -- Book VI, 510b-511b

narrative:
"How do you mean?"

"I mean, as I was saying, that arithmetic has a very great and elevating effect, compelling the soul to reason about abstract number, and rebelling against the introduction of visible or tangible objects into the argument. You know how steadily the masters of the art repel and ridicule any one who attempts to divide absolute unity when he is calculating, and if you divide, they multiply, taking care that one shall continue one and not become lost in fractions."
refashioned:
"What do you mean?" asked Glaucon.

"Arithmetic has a profoundly elevating effect," Socrates explained. "It compels the soul to reason about abstract number and resists any attempt to introduce visible or tangible objects into the argument. You know how mathematicians react when someone tries to divide absolute unity during calculation -- they immediately multiply it back, insisting that one must remain one and never dissolve into fractions."
elegant: Arithmetic compels the soul to reason abstractly, resisting the introduction of sensible objects. Mathematicians refuse to divide unity -- if you split one, they multiply it back, insisting that one must remain indivisible.
summary: Socrates explains that arithmetic elevates the soul by forcing abstract reasoning about number, rejecting visible or tangible objects. Mathematicians fiercely protect the indivisibility of unity -- if someone divides one, they multiply to restore it, ensuring one remains one.
distilled: Arithmetic's philosophical power lies in forcing the mind to reason abstractly, treating numbers as indivisible intelligible realities rather than divisible physical things.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- The mathematicians' refusal to divide unity reflects a deep Greek mathematical concern: the unit (monas) was considered indivisible; fractions were handled as ratios between whole numbers -- This connects to the Pythagorean tradition where numbers were fundamental realities -- 'Abstract number' vs. 'visible objects' = the distinction between intelligible and sensible, the upper and lower halves of the Divided Line -- Cross-ref: the Pythagorean influence on Plato's philosophy of mathematics; Aristotle's Metaphysics 987b on Plato's mathematical ontology

narrative:
"That is very true."

"Now, suppose a person were to say to them: O my friends, what are these wonderful numbers about which you are reasoning, in which, as you say, there is a unity such as you demand, and each unit is equal, invariable, indivisible, -- what would they answer?"

"They would answer, as I should conceive, that they were speaking of those numbers which can only be realised in thought."

"Then you see that this knowledge may be truly called necessary, necessitating as it clearly does the use of the pure intelligence in the attainment of pure truth?"

"Yes; that is a marked characteristic of it."

"And have you further observed, that those who have a natural talent for calculation are generally quick at every other kind of knowledge; and even the dull if they have had an arithmetical training, although they may derive no other advantage from it, always become much quicker than they would otherwise have been."

"Very true," he said.

"And indeed, you will not easily find a more difficult study, and not many as difficult."

"You will not."

"And, for all these reasons, arithmetic is a kind of knowledge in which the best natures should be trained, and which must not be given up."

"I agree."

"Let this then be made one of our subjects of education. And next, shall we enquire whether the kindred science also concerns us?"

"You mean geometry?"

"Exactly so."

"Clearly," he said, "we are concerned with that part of geometry which relates to war; for in pitching a camp, or taking up a position, or closing or extending the lines of an army, or any other military manoeuvre, whether in actual battle or on a march, it will make all the difference whether a general is or is not a geometrician."
refashioned:
"Very true," said Glaucon.

"Now suppose someone asked these mathematicians: 'What are these remarkable numbers you reason about -- these units you insist are equal, invariable, and indivisible? What would they say?'"

"They'd say they're speaking of numbers that can only exist in thought."

"Then you can see why this knowledge truly deserves to be called necessary -- it necessitates the use of pure intelligence to attain pure truth."

"Yes, that's clearly its distinguishing feature."

"And have you noticed that people with a talent for calculation tend to be quick at every other kind of learning? And even the slow ones, if trained in arithmetic, become sharper than they would have been otherwise -- even if they gain no other benefit."

"Very true."

"And you won't easily find a more challenging study."

"No, you won't."

"For all these reasons, the best minds should be trained in arithmetic, and it must not be neglected."

"I agree."

"Let arithmetic be our first subject, then. Now, shall we consider whether its sister science concerns us too?"

"You mean geometry?"

"Exactly."

"The part of geometry relevant to war is obvious," said Glaucon. "Pitching camps, taking positions, extending or contracting battle lines, maneuvering in combat or on the march -- a general who knows geometry has a huge advantage over one who doesn't."
elegant: Mathematicians acknowledge their numbers exist only in thought -- making arithmetic a discipline of pure intelligence pursuing pure truth. It sharpens all minds, even dull ones. Having established arithmetic as the first subject, Socrates turns to geometry, whose military applications Glaucon immediately recognizes.
summary: Mathematicians confirm their numbers exist only in thought, proving arithmetic trains pure intelligence for pure truth. It sharpens all minds and should be required for the best natures. With arithmetic established as the first subject, Socrates introduces geometry. Glaucon immediately sees its military applications in camp layout and troop movements.
distilled: Arithmetic trains pure thought for pure truth and sharpens every mind; geometry, the second subject, has obvious military applications but -- like arithmetic -- its philosophical value runs deeper.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Numbers which can only be realized in thought' = intelligible number, the Forms of numbers, not countable physical objects -- The claim that arithmetic sharpens ALL minds anticipates modern arguments for mathematical education -- Geometry is introduced as the second curriculum subject; Glaucon again focuses on practical/military use, which Socrates will redirect -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (mathematical objects occupy dianoia, Book VI 510b-511b); the curriculum sequence: arithmetic -> geometry -> solid geometry -> astronomy -> harmonics -> dialectic

narrative: "Yes," I said, "but for that purpose a very little of either geometry or calculation will be enough; the question relates rather to the greater and more advanced part of geometry -- whether that tends in any degree to make more easy the vision of the idea of good; and thither, as I was saying, all things tend which compel the soul to turn her gaze towards that place, where is the full perfection of being, which she ought, by all means, to behold."
refashioned: "Yes," said Socrates, "but for military purposes, only a small amount of geometry or calculation is needed. The real question is about the higher, more advanced part of geometry -- whether it helps make the vision of the Good more accessible. As I've been saying, everything that compels the soul to turn toward the place where being reaches its full perfection is what we're after. That is what the soul must, by all means, behold."
elegant: Only a little geometry is needed for military purposes. The real question is whether advanced geometry helps the soul apprehend the Form of the Good -- the full perfection of being that the soul must behold.
summary: Socrates dismisses the military applications of geometry as requiring only basic skills. The real question is whether advanced geometry compels the soul to turn toward the Form of the Good -- the full perfection of being.
distilled: The military value of geometry is trivial; what matters is whether its higher study turns the soul toward the vision of ultimate reality.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- Socrates consistently redirects Glaucon from practical applications to philosophical significance -- 'The idea of good' = the Form of the Good, the 'unhypothetical first principle' from Book VI -- 'All things tend' toward the Good echoes Book VI's claim that the Good is the cause of all truth and being -- Cross-ref: Book VI (505a-509c) on the Form of the Good; the curriculum's purpose is not practical skill but the soul's ascent

narrative:
"True," he said.

"Then if geometry compels us to view being, it concerns us; if becoming only, it does not concern us?"

"Yes, that is what we assert."

"Yet anybody who has the least acquaintance with geometry will not deny that such a conception of the science is in flat contradiction to the ordinary language of geometricians."

"How so?"

"They have in view practice only, and are always speaking, in a narrow and ridiculous manner, of squaring and extending and applying and the like -- they confuse the necessities of geometry with those of daily life; whereas knowledge is the real object of the whole science."

"Certainly," he said.

"Then must not a further admission be made?"

"What admission?"

"That the knowledge at which geometry aims is knowledge of the eternal, and not of aught perishing and transient."

"That," he replied, "may be readily allowed, and is true."

"Then, my noble friend, geometry will draw the soul towards truth, and create the spirit of philosophy, and raise up that which is now unhappily allowed to fall down."

"Nothing will be more likely to have such an effect."

"Then nothing should be more sternly laid down than that the inhabitants of your fair city should by all means learn geometry. Moreover the science has indirect effects, which are not small."

"Of what kind?" he said.

"There are the military advantages of which you spoke," I said; "and in all departments of knowledge, as experience proves, any one who has studied geometry is infinitely quicker of apprehension than one who has not."

"Yes indeed," he said, "there is an infinite difference between them."

"Then shall we propose this as a second branch of knowledge which our youth will study?"

"Let us do so," he replied.

"And suppose we make astronomy the third -- what do you say?"

"I am strongly inclined to it," he said; "the observation of the seasons and of months and years is as essential to the general as it is to the farmer or sailor."
refashioned:
"True," said Glaucon.

"So if geometry compels us to view being, it's relevant to our purpose. If it deals only with becoming, it isn't."

"That's our principle."

"But anyone who knows even a little geometry would say this conception flatly contradicts how geometers actually talk."

"How so?"

"They focus entirely on practice -- always talking in a narrow, ridiculous way about squaring, extending, applying, and so on. They confuse the demands of geometry with the demands of daily life, when knowledge is really the point of the entire science."

"Certainly."

"Then we must make a further admission."

"What?"

"That geometry ultimately aims at knowledge of the eternal -- not of anything that perishes or changes."

"That's readily granted, and true."

"Then geometry will draw the soul toward truth, cultivate the spirit of philosophy, and lift up what is now sadly allowed to sink down."

"Nothing is more likely to have that effect."

"Then we should firmly require that everyone in our city learn geometry. And beyond its philosophical value, it has significant secondary benefits."

"Like what?"

"The military advantages you mentioned. And in every field of knowledge, experience shows that anyone who has studied geometry grasps things infinitely faster than someone who hasn't."

"That's absolutely true -- the difference is enormous."

"Then geometry becomes the second branch of study for our youth?"

"Yes, let's establish that."

"And shall we make astronomy the third?"

"I'm strongly inclined to agree," said Glaucon. "Observing seasons, months, and years is as essential for a general as for a farmer or sailor."
elegant: Geometry aims at eternal knowledge, not practical applications like squaring and extending. It draws the soul toward truth and creates the philosophical spirit. Established as the second subject, Socrates then proposes astronomy as the third. Glaucon again focuses on practical value -- tracking seasons -- which Socrates will again redirect.
summary: Socrates argues that geometry, despite geometers' practical focus on squaring and extending, truly aims at knowledge of the eternal. It draws the soul toward truth and philosophy. Established as the second subject after arithmetic, astronomy is proposed as the third. Glaucon appreciates its practical value for tracking seasons, which Socrates will challenge.
distilled: Geometry aims not at practical construction but at knowledge of the eternal, drawing the soul toward truth and philosophy.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Squaring and extending and applying' = the practical language of working geometers, which Socrates dismisses as missing the philosophical point -- 'Knowledge of the eternal' = geometry studies Forms (the perfect circle, the ideal triangle) not physical shapes -- Glaucon's response to astronomy ('observation of seasons') again focuses on practical value, setting up Socrates' correction in 7.39 and 7.42-7.43 -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (geometers work with hypotheses and diagrams but point toward intelligible realities, Book VI 510b-511b); Meno 82b-86c (geometric proof as path to recollection)

narrative: "I am amused," I said, "at your fear of the world, which makes you guard against the appearance of insisting upon useless studies; and I quite admit the difficulty of believing that in every man there is an eye of the soul which, when by other pursuits lost and dimmed, is by these purified and re-illumined; and is more precious far than ten thousand bodily eyes, for by it alone is truth seen. Now there are two classes of persons: one class of those who will agree with you and will take your words as a revelation; another class to whom they will be utterly unmeaning, and who will naturally deem them to be idle tales, for they see no sort of profit which is to be obtained from them. And therefore you had better decide at once with which of the two you are proposing to argue. You will very likely say with neither, and that your chief aim in carrying on the argument is your own improvement; at the same time you do not grudge to others any benefit which they may receive."
refashioned:
"I'm amused," said Socrates, "by your concern about public opinion -- your eagerness to avoid seeming to champion useless studies. I grant it's hard to believe that every person has an eye of the soul which, when dimmed and lost through other pursuits, can be purified and re-illuminated by these studies. Yet this eye is worth far more than ten thousand bodily eyes, because truth can only be seen through it.

"Now, there are two kinds of people: those who will hear this and receive it as revelation, and those who will find it meaningless -- idle tales with no practical profit. So you'd better decide which group you're addressing. You'll probably say neither -- that your real aim in pursuing this argument is your own improvement, while not begrudging others whatever benefit they might gain."
elegant: Socrates gently mocks Glaucon's concern about appearing impractical. Every soul has an inner eye, dimmed by worldly pursuits but purifiable through these studies -- worth more than ten thousand bodily eyes, for it alone sees truth. Some will receive this as revelation; others will dismiss it as useless.
summary: Socrates teases Glaucon for worrying about seeming impractical and affirms that every soul has an inner eye -- dimmed by other pursuits but re-illuminated by these studies -- worth more than ten thousand physical eyes because it alone perceives truth. People divide into those who find this revelatory and those who find it meaningless.
distilled: The eye of the soul, worth more than ten thousand bodily eyes, is dimmed by worldly pursuits and re-illuminated by philosophical study -- but only some will believe this.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Eye of the soul' (tes psyches omma) is one of Plato's most powerful metaphors, used also at 533d -- The valuation of inner sight over physical sight connects to the Cave allegory: the freed prisoner's physical eyes suffer, but his soul's eye is opened -- 'Ten thousand bodily eyes' -- a characteristically Platonic hyperbole -- The two classes of listeners anticipates the divided reception of philosophy throughout history -- Cross-ref: Book VI (508d) where the Good illuminates the intelligible world as the sun illuminates the visible; Phaedrus 247c-d (the soul's vision of the Forms)

narrative:
"I think that I should prefer to carry on the argument mainly on my own behalf."

"Then take a step backward, for we have gone wrong in the order of the sciences."

"What was the mistake?" he said.

"After plane geometry," I said, "we proceeded at once to solids in revolution, instead of taking solids in themselves; whereas after the second dimension the third, which is concerned with cubes and dimensions of depth, ought to have followed."

"That is true, Socrates; but so little seems to be known as yet about these subjects."
refashioned:
"I'd rather pursue the argument mainly for my own sake," said Glaucon.

"Then let's step back," Socrates said, "because we've gotten the order of the sciences wrong."

"What was the mistake?"

"After plane geometry -- two dimensions -- we jumped straight to solids in motion, which is astronomy. But we should have first studied solids in themselves -- the third dimension, concerned with cubes and depth."

"That's true, Socrates, but so little seems to be known about solid geometry yet."
elegant: Socrates corrects the curriculum order: after plane geometry (two dimensions), solid geometry (three dimensions -- cubes and depth) should come before astronomy (solids in motion). But Glaucon notes the field is barely developed.
summary: Socrates catches a mistake in their curriculum ordering: after plane geometry, they should study solid geometry (three-dimensional figures) before astronomy (solids in motion). Glaucon agrees but notes that solid geometry is still a poorly developed field.
distilled: The proper order of mathematical education proceeds by dimension: plane geometry, then solid geometry, then the motion of solids (astronomy).
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Solids in revolution' = astronomy, the study of moving three-dimensional bodies -- The correction reveals Plato's systematic approach: education should follow the logical order of mathematical objects (1D -> 2D -> 3D -> 3D in motion) -- Solid geometry (stereometry) was indeed underdeveloped in Plato's time; Theaetetus and Eudoxus made important advances -- Cross-ref: this passage influenced the structure of Euclid's Elements (Books XI-XIII on solid geometry); the curriculum: arithmetic -> plane geometry -> solid geometry -> astronomy -> harmonics -> dialectic

narrative: "Why, yes," I said, "and for two reasons: -- in the first place, no government patronises them; this leads to a want of energy in the pursuit of them, and they are difficult; in the second place, students cannot learn them unless they have a director. But then a director can hardly be found, and even if he could, as matters now stand, the students, who are very conceited, would not attend to him. That, however, would be otherwise if the whole State became the director of these studies and gave honour to them; then disciples would want to come, and there would be continuous and earnest search, and discoveries would be made; since even now, disregarded as they are by the world, and maimed of their fair proportions, and although none of their votaries can tell the use of them, still these studies force their way by their natural charm, and very likely, if they had the help of the State, they would some day emerge into light."
refashioned:
"Yes," said Socrates, "and for two reasons. First, no government funds or supports these studies, so there's no energy behind pursuing them -- and they're inherently difficult. Second, students can't learn without a teacher, but qualified teachers are nearly impossible to find. And even if one appeared, today's students are too arrogant to listen.

"But things would be different if the state itself directed these studies and honored them. Students would flock to learn, research would be sustained and serious, and discoveries would follow. Even now, neglected by the world, stunted in their development, with nobody able to explain their usefulness, these studies push forward by sheer intellectual charm. With state support, they would surely come into their own."
elegant: Solid geometry languishes because no government supports it and qualified teachers are scarce. Yet even neglected, these studies advance by their natural intellectual charm. With state patronage, they would flourish and yield discoveries.
summary: Socrates identifies why solid geometry remains undeveloped: lack of government support and scarcity of qualified teachers. Yet even neglected and dismissed as useless, these studies advance through their inherent intellectual appeal. State patronage would transform the field.
distilled: Without state support, even naturally compelling studies languish -- but their inherent intellectual charm ensures they advance despite neglect.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- This is a remarkable passage about research funding and institutional support for pure science -- 'No government patronises them' = a complaint about the lack of state investment in theoretical research, strikingly modern -- 'Force their way by their natural charm' suggests mathematical truth has an inherent attractiveness that survives institutional neglect -- Cross-ref: this passage may reflect conditions at Plato's Academy, where stereometry was being actively developed; Theaetetus and Eudoxus of Cnidus were key figures

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "there is a remarkable charm in them. But I do not clearly understand the change in the order. First you began with a geometry of plane surfaces?"

"Yes," I said.

"And you placed astronomy next, and then you made a step backward?"

"Yes, and I have delayed you by my hurry; the ludicrous state of solid geometry, which, in natural order, should have followed, made me pass over this branch and go on to astronomy, or motion of solids."

"True," he said.

"Then assuming that the science now omitted would come into existence if encouraged by the State, let us go on to astronomy, which will be fourth."

"The right order," he replied.

"And now, Socrates, as you rebuked the vulgar manner in which I praised astronomy before, my praise shall be given in your own spirit. For every one, as I think, must see that astronomy compels the soul to look upwards and leads us from this world to another."

"Every one but myself," I said; "to every one else this may be clear, but not to me."

"And what then would you say?"

"I should rather say that those who elevate astronomy into philosophy appear to me to make us look downwards and not upwards."
refashioned:
"Yes," said Glaucon, "they certainly have a charm. But I'm confused about the order. You started with plane geometry?"

"Yes."

"Then placed astronomy next, and now you've stepped backward?"

"Right -- I rushed ahead. The sad state of solid geometry, which logically should come next, made me skip it and jump to astronomy, the study of solids in motion."

"I see."

"So let's assume the state will develop solid geometry in due time, and proceed to astronomy as our fourth subject."

"That's the right order," Glaucon agreed. "And now, Socrates, since you criticized my earlier praise of astronomy as too vulgar, let me try again in your spirit. Surely everyone can see that astronomy compels the soul to look upward and leads us from this world to a higher one."

"Everyone except me," said Socrates. "That may be clear to everyone else, but not to me."

"What would you say instead?"

"I'd say that those who elevate astronomy into philosophy this way actually make us look downward, not upward."
elegant: With solid geometry assumed as the third subject, astronomy becomes the fourth. Glaucon praises astronomy for compelling the soul to look upward. Socrates paradoxically counters: popular astronomy makes us look downward, not upward.
summary: Having placed solid geometry third (assuming state support), Socrates makes astronomy fourth. When Glaucon praises astronomy for turning the soul upward, Socrates paradoxically replies that the way most people approach astronomy actually directs the soul downward, not upward.
distilled: Simply gazing at the physical sky is not true philosophical astronomy -- staring upward at sensible objects can actually direct the soul downward.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- Socrates' paradox ('looking upward makes you look downward') captures his key educational principle: the direction of the body's eyes is irrelevant; what matters is the direction of the soul's eye -- Glaucon's praise of astronomy for 'looking up' is literally correct but philosophically naive -- Cross-ref: the four subjects in order: arithmetic, plane geometry, solid geometry, astronomy; the distinction between sensible and intelligible astronomy will be developed in 7.43-7.47

narrative:
"What do you mean?" he asked.

"You," I replied, "have in your mind a truly sublime conception of our knowledge of the things above. And I dare say that if a person were to throw his head back and study the fretted ceiling, you would still think that his mind was the percipient, and not his eyes. And you are very likely right, and I may be a simpleton: but, in my opinion, that knowledge only which is of being and of the unseen can make the soul look upwards, and whether a man gapes at the heavens or blinks on the ground, seeking to learn some particular of sense, I would deny that he can learn, for nothing of that sort is matter of science; his soul is looking downwards, not upwards, whether his way to knowledge is by water or by land, whether he floats, or only lies on his back."

"I acknowledge," he said, "the justice of your rebuke. Still, I should like to ascertain how astronomy can be learned in any manner more conducive to that knowledge of which we are speaking?"
refashioned:
"What do you mean?" Glaucon asked.

"You have a very elevated idea of studying 'things above,'" Socrates replied. "I suspect that if someone threw their head back to study a decorated ceiling, you'd say their mind was doing the perceiving, not their eyes. You may well be right, and I may be a simpleton. But in my view, only knowledge of being and the unseen can truly make the soul look upward. Whether someone gapes at the sky or stares at the ground trying to learn some fact of sense experience, I would deny that they are learning at all -- because sense particulars are not the stuff of science. Their soul is looking downward, not upward, whether they study by sea or by land, floating or lying on their back."

"I accept the justice of your rebuke," Glaucon said. "But then how should astronomy be studied in a way that leads to the knowledge we're after?"
elegant: Only knowledge of being and the unseen makes the soul look upward. Whether you gape at the sky or stare at the ground, if you're seeking sensory particulars, your soul is looking downward. Sense experience is not science.
summary: Socrates argues that physical direction is irrelevant -- only knowledge of being and the unseen turns the soul upward. Whether gazing at the sky or the ground, seeking sense particulars means the soul looks downward. Glaucon accepts the rebuke and asks how astronomy should properly be studied.
distilled: True intellectual ascent has nothing to do with physical direction -- staring at the sky seeking sense data is just as 'downward' as staring at the ground.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'fretted ceiling' example is both humorous and pointed: physical upwardness does not equal intellectual upwardness -- 'Nothing of that sort is matter of science' (episteme) = sensory particulars cannot ground genuine knowledge -- 'Whether he floats or lies on his back' is likely a jab at observational astronomers -- This passage establishes the distinction between empirical and rational astronomy that will be developed in 7.44-7.47 -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 65a-67b (the senses as impediments to knowledge); the Divided Line's distinction between dianoia and eikasia

narrative: "I will tell you," I said: "The starry heaven which we behold is wrought upon a visible ground, and therefore, although the fairest and most perfect of visible things, must necessarily be deemed inferior far to the true motions of absolute swiftness and absolute slowness, which are relative to each other, and carry with them that which is contained in them, in the true number and in every true figure. Now, these are to be apprehended by reason and intelligence, but not by sight."
refashioned: "Let me explain," said Socrates. "The starry sky we see is a physical, visible thing. Although it's the most beautiful and perfect of all visible objects, it is necessarily far inferior to the true motions -- the ideal relationships of absolute speed and absolute slowness, moving in true number and perfect geometric figure. These true motions carry within them all that truly belongs to them. But they can only be grasped by reason and intelligence, never by sight."
elegant: The visible sky, though the fairest of physical things, is far inferior to the true motions of absolute speed and slowness in perfect mathematical proportion. These intelligible motions are grasped by reason, not sight.
summary: Socrates explains that the visible starry sky, though the most beautiful physical object, is far inferior to the true astronomical motions -- ideal relationships of speed, slowness, number, and figure that can only be apprehended by reason, not by the eyes.
distilled: The visible heavens, however beautiful, are merely imperfect copies of the true mathematical motions that only reason can apprehend.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Absolute swiftness and absolute slowness' = the intelligible mathematical ratios governing celestial motion, the Forms behind observable astronomy -- 'True number and every true figure' = the mathematical structures underlying astronomical phenomena -- This passage establishes Plato's distinction between empirical and rational astronomy, which profoundly influenced later astronomical theory -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 37d-39e (the cosmos as a moving image of eternity); the Divided Line places sensible astronomical observations below the mathematical reasoning they should serve

narrative:
"True," he replied.

"The spangled heavens should be used as a pattern and with a view to that higher knowledge; their beauty is like the beauty of figures or pictures excellently wrought by the hand of Daedalus, or some other great artist, which we may chance to behold; any geometrician who saw them would appreciate the exquisiteness of their workmanship, but he would never dream of thinking that in them he could find the true equal or the true double, or the truth of any other proportion."
refashioned:
"True," Glaucon replied.

"The starry sky should be used as a model pointing toward higher knowledge," Socrates continued. "Its beauty is like that of diagrams or pictures superbly crafted by Daedalus or some other master artist. A geometer who saw such artwork would admire the exquisite workmanship, but would never imagine that these drawings could reveal the true equal, the true double, or the truth of any mathematical proportion."
elegant: The visible heavens should serve as pointers toward higher mathematical knowledge, just as a geometer admires Daedalus's beautiful diagrams without mistaking them for the mathematical truths they illustrate.
summary: Socrates compares the visible heavens to beautiful diagrams by Daedalus: a geometer would admire their craftsmanship but never mistake them for the mathematical truths they represent. Similarly, the starry sky should point toward higher mathematical knowledge, not be treated as the truth itself.
distilled: The visible heavens are beautiful illustrations of mathematical truth, not the truth itself -- just as a perfect diagram is not the theorem it represents.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- Daedalus was the legendary master craftsman of Greek mythology, builder of the Labyrinth and creator of lifelike statues -- The analogy between astronomical observation and geometric diagrams is precise: both are sensible representations of intelligible truths -- 'The true equal or the true double' = mathematical Forms, perfectly precise relationships that physical objects can only approximate -- Cross-ref: Meno 97d-98a (Daedalus's statues that could move); this passage influenced the mathematical astronomy of Eudoxus and later Ptolemy

narrative:
"No," he replied, "such an idea would be ridiculous."

"And will not a true astronomer have the same feeling when he looks at the movements of the stars? Will he not think that heaven and the things in heaven are framed by the Creator of them in the most perfect manner? But he will never imagine that the proportions of night and day, or of both to the month, or of the month to the year, or of the stars to these and to one another, and any other things that are material and visible can also be eternal and subject to no deviation -- that would be absurd; and it is equally absurd to take so much pains in investigating their exact truth."
refashioned:
"No," said Glaucon, "that would be ridiculous."

"And won't a true astronomer feel the same way about the movements of the stars?" Socrates asked. "He'll recognize that the heavens are crafted by their creator as perfectly as possible. But he'll never imagine that the proportions of night to day, or day to month, or month to year, or the relations of stars to each other -- or anything material and visible -- can be truly eternal and perfectly invariable. That would be absurd. And it's equally absurd to spend enormous effort trying to determine their exact empirical truth."
elegant: A true astronomer admires the Creator's work but knows that material, visible proportions -- night to day, month to year -- cannot be perfectly eternal or invariable. It is absurd to labor over their exact empirical measurement as though they were.
summary: Socrates argues that a genuine astronomer will admire the heavens as divinely crafted but never mistake their visible proportions for eternal truths. Material ratios of night, day, months, and years cannot be perfectly invariable, and obsessive empirical measurement of them is misguided.
distilled: The visible heavens are beautifully made but imperfect -- a true astronomer studies mathematical principles, not the precise measurements of material phenomena.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Framed by the Creator' (demiourgos) foreshadows the Timaeus where the Demiurge creates the cosmos as an image of the eternal -- The claim that visible astronomical ratios cannot be perfectly eternal anticipates the distinction between ideal mathematical models and observational data -- 'Equally absurd to take so much pains' -- a provocative claim that pure empirical astronomy is misguided -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 28a-29d (the cosmos as an image of the eternal model); this passage influenced debates about the relationship between mathematical models and observations throughout the history of science

narrative:
"I quite agree, though I never thought of this before."

"Then," I said, "in astronomy, as in geometry, we should employ problems, and let the heavens alone if we would approach the subject in the right way and so make the natural gift of reason to be of any real use."

"That," he said, "is a work infinitely beyond our present astronomers."

"Yes," I said; "and there are many other things which must also have a similar extension given to them, if our legislation is to be of any value. But can you tell me of any other suitable study?"

"No," he said, "not without thinking."

"Motion," I said, "has many forms, and not one only; two of them are obvious enough even to wits no better than ours; and there are others, as I imagine, which may be left to wiser persons."

"But where are the two?"

"There is a second," I said, "which is the counterpart of the one already named."

"And what may that be?"

"The second," I said, "would seem relatively to the ears to be what the first is to the eyes; for I conceive that as the eyes are designed to look up at the stars, so are the ears to hear harmonious motions; and these are sister sciences -- as the Pythagoreans say, and we, Glaucon, agree with them?"

"Yes," he replied.

"But this," I said, "is a laborious study, and therefore we had better go and learn of them; and they will tell us whether there are any other applications of these sciences. At the same time, we must not lose sight of our own higher object."

"What is that?"

"There is a perfection which all knowledge ought to reach, and which our pupils ought also to attain, and not to fall short of, as I was saying that they did in astronomy. For in the science of harmony, as you probably know, the same thing happens. The teachers of harmony compare the sounds and consonances which are heard only, and their labour, like that of the astronomers, is in vain."
refashioned:
"I completely agree, though I'd never considered this before."

"So in astronomy, as in geometry," Socrates said, "we should work with mathematical problems and leave the physical heavens alone -- that's the right approach and the only way to make genuine use of our rational faculty."

"That's infinitely beyond what our current astronomers do."

"True. And many other fields need the same kind of reform if our educational legislation is to have any value. Can you think of another suitable subject?"

"Not offhand."

"Consider this: motion has many forms, not just one. Two are obvious even to ordinary minds; others can be left to wiser people."

"What are the two?"

"The second is the counterpart of the first -- astronomy."

"What is it?"

"As astronomy relates to the eyes, this second science relates to the ears. As the eyes perceive celestial motion, the ears perceive harmonious motion. These are sister sciences -- as the Pythagoreans say, and as we agree."

"Yes."

"But harmonics is a laborious study, so we should consult the Pythagoreans about it and about any further applications. Meanwhile, we must keep our higher purpose in view."

"What purpose?"

"Every science should reach a certain perfection, and our students must attain it. In harmonics, the same error occurs as in astronomy. Music theorists compare sounds and consonances that they merely hear, and their labor -- like that of empirical astronomers -- is ultimately in vain."
elegant: Astronomy should work with mathematical problems, not physical observation. Its sister science is harmonics -- what astronomy is to the eyes, harmonics is to the ears. But music theorists, like empirical astronomers, make the same mistake: they compare heard sounds rather than seeking the mathematical principles behind them.
summary: Socrates prescribes mathematical problem-solving in astronomy rather than observation, then introduces harmonics as astronomy's sister science -- relating to ears as astronomy relates to eyes, per Pythagorean teaching. But harmonics suffers the same error as empirical astronomy: theorists study heard sounds rather than the mathematical principles of harmony.
distilled: Astronomy and harmonics are sister sciences of motion -- one visual, one auditory -- but both must be pursued mathematically rather than empirically to reach genuine knowledge.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'As the Pythagoreans say' -- explicit acknowledgment of Pythagorean influence; Pythagoras discovered the mathematical ratios of musical intervals -- Astronomy = motion perceived by eyes; harmonics = motion perceived by ears; both are studies of mathematical proportion -- 'Let the heavens alone' is Plato's most radical statement about the method of astronomy: study mathematical problems, not observable phenomena -- The curriculum is now: arithmetic, plane geometry, solid geometry, astronomy, harmonics -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 35b-36d (the World Soul structured by musical ratios); the Pythagorean tradition of mathematical harmonics

narrative: "Yes, by heaven!" he said; "and 'tis as good as a play to hear them talking about their condensed notes, as they call them; they put their ears close alongside of the strings like persons catching a sound from their neighbour's wall -- one set of them declaring that they distinguish an intermediate note and have found the least interval which should be the unit of measurement; the others insisting that the two sounds have passed into the same -- either party setting their ears before their understanding."
refashioned: "Yes, by heaven!" Glaucon exclaimed. "And it's like watching a comedy to see them debating their 'condensed notes,' as they call them. They press their ears right up against the strings like people eavesdropping through a neighbor's wall -- one group claiming they can detect an intermediate tone and have found the smallest interval to use as a unit of measurement, the other insisting the two sounds are identical. Both sides put their ears ahead of their understanding."
elegant: Glaucon describes empirical music theorists as comical: pressing ears to strings like eavesdroppers, quarreling over tiny intervals, always prioritizing what they hear over what they understand.
summary: Glaucon vividly describes the absurdity of empirical music theorists who press their ears against strings arguing about microtones, some claiming to hear intermediate notes, others insisting two tones are the same -- all of them prioritizing hearing over understanding.
distilled: Empirical music theorists are absurd, pressing their ears to strings and arguing over what they hear rather than seeking mathematical understanding.
notes: Glaucon is speaking -- This passage satirizes the empirical school of harmonics (likely associated with Aristoxenus's predecessors), who sought to determine intervals by ear alone -- 'Condensed notes' (pyknomata) = microtonal intervals, a technical term in Greek music theory -- 'Setting their ears before their understanding' captures the fundamental Platonic critique: sense experience without rational analysis is not knowledge -- The contrast is between empirical harmonics (listening) and mathematical harmonics (reasoning about numerical ratios) -- Cross-ref: Philolaus and the Pythagorean mathematical approach to music vs. the empiricists

narrative: "You mean," I said, "those gentlemen who tease and torture the strings and rack them on the pegs of the instrument: I might carry on the metaphor and speak after their manner of the blows which the plectrum gives, and make accusations against the strings, both of backwardness and forwardness to sound; but this would be tedious, and therefore I will only say that these are not the men, and that I am referring to the Pythagoreans, of whom I was just now proposing to enquire about harmony. For they too are in error, like the astronomers; they investigate the numbers of the harmonies which are heard, but they never attain to problems -- that is to say, they never reach the natural harmonies of number, or reflect why some numbers are harmonious and others not."
refashioned:
"You're describing the experimentalists," Socrates said, "who torture strings on the pegs of their instruments. I could extend the metaphor -- talk about the blows of the plectrum, accuse the strings of being too eager or too reluctant to sound -- but that would be tedious.

"Those aren't the people I mean. I'm talking about the Pythagoreans, whom I proposed we consult about harmonics. They too are in error, like the empirical astronomers. They investigate the numerical ratios of the harmonies they hear, but they never reach the level of genuine problems -- they never discover the natural harmonies of number itself, or ask why some numbers are harmonious and others are not."
elegant: Even the Pythagoreans, despite their mathematical approach to music, fall short. They investigate the numerical ratios of heard harmonies but never ask the deeper question: why are some numbers inherently harmonious and others not?
summary: Socrates distinguishes between the string-torturing experimentalists and the mathematically-minded Pythagoreans. Even the Pythagoreans fall short: they study the numerical ratios of audible harmonies but never reach the deeper question of why some numbers are inherently harmonious and others are not.
distilled: Even the Pythagoreans, who approach music mathematically, fail to ask the truly philosophical question: why are some numbers harmonious by nature?
notes: Socrates is speaking -- The critique of the Pythagoreans is significant: even their mathematical approach to music doesn't go far enough because they remain tied to audible phenomena -- 'Natural harmonies of number' = the intelligible mathematical properties that make certain ratios consonant -- The distinction between empiricists (who torture strings) and Pythagoreans (who count ratios) and Plato's ideal (who seek the formal principles behind harmony) represents three levels of inquiry -- Cross-ref: the Divided Line's hierarchy; Pythagorean music theory discovered ratios like 2:1 (octave), 3:2 (fifth), 4:3 (fourth); Philebus 17c-e on the science of intervals

narrative:
"That," he said, "is a thing of more than mortal knowledge."

"A thing," I replied, "which I would rather call useful; that is, if sought after with a view to the beautiful and good; but if pursued in any other spirit, useless."

"Very true," he said.

"Now, when all these studies reach the point of inter-communion and connection with one another, and come to be considered in their mutual affinities, then, I think, but not till then, will the pursuit of them have a value for our objects; otherwise there is no profit in them."

"I suspect so; but you are speaking, Socrates, of a vast work."

"What do you mean?" I said; "the prelude or what? Do you not know that all this is but the prelude to the actual strain which we have to learn? For you surely would not regard the skilled mathematician as a dialectician?"

"Assuredly not," he said; "I have hardly ever known a mathematician who was capable of reasoning."

"But do you imagine that men who are unable to give and take a reason will have the knowledge which we require of them?"
refashioned:
"That sounds like more than human knowledge," said Glaucon.

"I'd call it useful rather than superhuman," Socrates replied, "-- useful if pursued with a view to the beautiful and the good. Pursued in any other spirit, it's useless."

"Very true."

"Now here's the crucial point: all these studies only become valuable for our purpose when they reach the stage of interconnection -- when their mutual relationships and affinities are understood. Until then, there's no real profit in them."

"I can see that, but you're describing an enormous undertaking, Socrates."

"What do you mean -- the vast work? This is merely the prelude! Don't you realize that everything we've discussed is just the overture to the main theme we still need to learn? Surely you wouldn't call a skilled mathematician a dialectician?"

"Certainly not," Glaucon said. "I've hardly ever met a mathematician capable of genuine philosophical reasoning."

"And can you imagine that people who can't give and receive rational arguments will possess the knowledge we require?"
elegant: All the mathematical sciences only become valuable when their interconnections are understood. But even then, they are merely the prelude. The real subject is dialectic. Mathematicians are rarely capable of genuine philosophical reasoning.
summary: Socrates declares that the mathematical sciences gain value only when their interconnections are understood -- but even then, they are merely a prelude. The real subject, dialectic, still awaits. Glaucon notes that mathematicians are rarely capable of genuine reasoning. Socrates insists that the ability to give and take rational arguments is essential.
distilled: All mathematical sciences are merely the prelude to dialectic -- and even skilled mathematicians rarely possess the capacity for genuine philosophical reasoning.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Prelude to the actual strain' (prooimion vs. nomos) = the mathematical curriculum is only preparation for dialectic, the crown of education -- The musical metaphor (prelude/strain) is apt given they've just discussed harmonics -- 'I have hardly ever known a mathematician who was capable of reasoning' -- a striking claim that mathematical skill doesn't guarantee philosophical ability -- 'Give and take a reason' (logon didonai kai dechesthai) is Plato's definition of dialectical competence -- Cross-ref: Book VI (510b-511d) where mathematics is placed below dialectic because it relies on unexamined hypotheses; the Divided Line's distinction between dianoia and noesis

narrative:
"Neither can this be supposed."

"And so, Glaucon," I said, "we have at last arrived at the hymn of dialectic. This is that strain which is of the intellect only, but which the faculty of sight will nevertheless be found to imitate; for sight, as you may remember, was imagined by us after a while to behold the real animals and stars, and last of all the sun himself. And so with dialectic; when a person starts on the discovery of the absolute by the light of reason only, and without any assistance of sense, and perseveres until by pure intelligence he arrives at the perception of the absolute good, he at last finds himself at the end of the intellectual world, as in the case of sight at the end of the visible."
refashioned:
"No, it cannot."

"And so, Glaucon," said Socrates, "we've finally arrived at the main theme -- the hymn of dialectic. This is the discipline of pure intellect alone, though the faculty of sight provides an analogy. Remember how in our allegory, sight eventually progressed to beholding real animals, then stars, and finally the sun itself? Dialectic follows the same pattern: when someone sets out to discover the absolute through reason alone, without any help from the senses, and perseveres until pure intelligence reaches the perception of the absolute good -- that person arrives at the summit of the intelligible world, just as sight arrives at the summit of the visible world."
elegant: Dialectic is the hymn of pure intellect. As sight in the allegory progressed from shadows to the sun, dialectic progresses through pure reason -- without sensory assistance -- until it arrives at the absolute good, the summit of the intelligible world.
summary: Socrates announces the arrival at dialectic, the crown of the curriculum -- pure intellect operating without sensory assistance. As the freed prisoner's sight eventually reached the sun, dialectic progresses through reason alone until it perceives the absolute good, the summit of the intelligible world.
distilled: Dialectic is the culmination of all education -- pure reason ascending without sensory aid to the perception of the absolute good.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'The hymn of dialectic' (nomos) = the main composition, following the mathematical 'prelude' (prooimion) -- Dialectic corresponds to noesis, the highest segment of the Divided Line (Book VI, 511b-e) -- 'Without any assistance of sense' = pure intellectual inquiry, no empirical data, no physical diagrams -- The parallel between sight's ascent (Cave allegory) and reason's ascent (dialectic) unifies the two great images of Book VII -- Cross-ref: Cave allegory (7.1-7.9); Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); Symposium 210a-212a (Diotima's ascent to absolute Beauty follows the same pattern)

narrative:
"Exactly," he said.

"Then this is the progress which you call dialectic?"

"True."

"But the release of the prisoners from chains, and their translation from the shadows to the images and to the light, and the ascent from the underground den to the sun, while in his presence they are vainly trying to look on animals and plants and the light of the sun, but are able to perceive even with their weak eyes the images in the water (which are divine), and are the shadows of true existence (not shadows of images cast by a light of fire, which compared with the sun is only an image) -- this power of elevating the highest principle in the soul to the contemplation of that which is best in existence, with which we may compare the raising of that faculty which is the very light of the body to the sight of that which is brightest in the material and visible world -- this power is given, as I was saying, by all that study and pursuit of the arts which has been described."
refashioned:
"Exactly," said Glaucon. "So that progress is what you call dialectic."

"Yes. But consider the earlier stages of the allegory: the prisoners freed from chains, their movement from shadows to the actual objects and toward the light, the ascent from the underground cave to the sunlit world above. There, even with weak eyes, they can perceive reflections in water -- which are divine shadows of true reality, not shadows of artificial objects cast by firelight (which is itself only a copy of the sun).

"The power to elevate the soul's highest principle to contemplate the best in existence -- comparable to raising the body's faculty of sight to perceive the brightest thing in the visible world -- this power is what all the mathematical studies and arts we've described provide."
elegant: The mathematical curriculum provides the power to elevate the soul's highest faculty to contemplate ultimate reality -- corresponding to the cave allegory's progression from chains and shadows through reflections and objects to the sun itself.
summary: Socrates connects the Cave allegory to the educational curriculum. The entire mathematical program -- arithmetic through harmonics -- provides the power to elevate the soul's highest principle to contemplate the greatest reality, just as the freed prisoner's eyes gradually adjust from shadows to the sun.
distilled: The mathematical sciences are the means by which the soul's highest faculty is gradually elevated from shadow to sunlight -- from becoming to the contemplation of ultimate being.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- This passage explicitly maps the mathematical curriculum onto the stages of the Cave allegory -- 'Images in the water which are divine' = mathematical objects, which are reflections of the Forms but more real than sensory appearances -- 'Shadows of images cast by firelight' = the lowest level of reality (sensory images of physical objects) -- The hierarchy: firelight shadows < physical objects < reflections in sunlight < the sun = shadows of images < images < mathematical objects < Forms < the Good -- Cross-ref: Cave allegory (7.1-7.9); Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); the mathematical curriculum maps onto the dianoia section of the Line

narrative: "I agree in what you are saying," he replied, "which may be hard to believe, yet, from another point of view, is harder still to deny. This, however, is not a theme to be treated of in passing only, but will have to be discussed again and again. And so, whether our conclusion be true or false, let us assume all this, and proceed at once from the prelude or preamble to the chief strain, and describe that in like manner. Say, then, what is the nature and what are the divisions of dialectic, and what are the paths which lead thither; for these paths will also lead to our final rest?"
refashioned: "I agree with what you're saying," Glaucon replied. "It's hard to believe, and yet even harder to deny. But this is not a subject for passing treatment -- it needs to be discussed again and again. So, whether our conclusions are correct or not, let's accept them for now and move from the prelude to the main theme. Tell me: what is the nature of dialectic? What are its divisions? And what are the paths that lead to it? Because those paths will also lead to our final destination -- our place of rest."
elegant: Glaucon finds the argument hard to believe yet harder to deny. He asks Socrates to describe dialectic itself: its nature, divisions, and the paths leading to it -- for those paths lead to the soul's final rest.
summary: Glaucon acknowledges the argument is difficult to accept yet impossible to refute. He asks Socrates to move from the mathematical prelude to the main subject: the nature, divisions, and pathways of dialectic -- which will lead to the soul's ultimate destination.
distilled: The argument for dialectic as the crown of knowledge is hard to believe but harder to deny -- now Glaucon demands its full description.
notes: Glaucon is speaking -- 'Hard to believe yet harder to deny' captures the characteristic response to Platonic metaphysics -- 'Our final rest' (anapaula) = the contemplation of the Good as the soul's ultimate fulfillment -- 'Discussed again and again' = Plato acknowledging that these matters require repeated investigation across multiple dialogues -- Cross-ref: the Phaedrus, Sophist, and Parmenides all continue investigating dialectic's nature; the Philebus explores the Good further

narrative:
"Dear Glaucon," I said, "you will not be able to follow me here, though I would do my best, and you should behold not an image only but the absolute truth, according to my notion. Whether what I told you would or would not have been a reality I cannot venture to say; but you would have seen something like reality; of that I am confident."

"Doubtless," he replied.

"But I must also remind you, that the power of dialectic alone can reveal this, and only to one who is a disciple of the previous sciences."

"Of that assertion you may be as confident as of the last."
refashioned:
"Dear Glaucon," said Socrates, "you won't be able to follow me all the way here, though I would do my best. Ideally, you would see not just an image but absolute truth itself -- or at least my understanding of it. Whether what I'd show you would be the actual reality, I can't say for certain. But you would see something resembling reality -- of that I'm confident."

"I'm sure of it," Glaucon replied.

"But I must remind you: only the power of dialectic can reveal this truth, and only to someone who has mastered the preliminary sciences."

"I'm as confident of that as of everything else you've said."
elegant: Socrates warns that Glaucon cannot fully follow where dialectic leads -- to absolute truth, not mere images. Only dialectic can reveal this, and only to those who have mastered the mathematical sciences.
summary: Socrates warns Glaucon he cannot fully follow the discussion of dialectic. Ideally one would see absolute truth, not images -- but only dialectic reveals this, and only to those who have completed the mathematical curriculum.
distilled: Dialectic reveals absolute truth -- but only to those disciplined by the preparatory sciences; without them, the soul cannot follow.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'You will not be able to follow me' = Socrates acknowledges the limits of discursive communication for the highest truths -- This echoes the Seventh Letter (341c-d) where Plato says the highest knowledge 'cannot be put into words' -- 'Not an image only but the absolute truth' = the difference between dianoia (mathematical images/hypotheses) and noesis (direct intellectual apprehension) -- 'A disciple of the previous sciences' = the mathematical curriculum is a prerequisite, not optional -- Cross-ref: Seventh Letter 341c-d; Book VI (511b-e) on dialectic's method of ascending beyond hypotheses

narrative: "And assuredly no one will argue that there is any other method of comprehending by any regular process all true existence or of ascertaining what each thing is in its own nature; for the arts in general are concerned with the desires or opinions of men, or are cultivated with a view to production and construction, or for the preservation of such productions and constructions; and as to the mathematical sciences which, as we were saying, have some apprehension of true being -- geometry and the like -- they only dream about being, but never can they behold the waking reality so long as they leave the hypotheses which they use unexamined, and are unable to give an account of them. For when a man knows not his own first principle, and when the conclusion and intermediate steps are also constructed out of he knows not what, how can he imagine that such a fabric of convention can ever become science?"
refashioned:
"And no one can seriously argue," Socrates continued, "that any other method systematically comprehends all true existence or determines what each thing is in its own nature. The arts generally deal with human desires, opinions, production, or the preservation of what's been produced. And the mathematical sciences -- geometry and the rest -- which we said do apprehend being to some degree? They merely dream about being. They can never behold waking reality as long as they leave their foundational hypotheses unexamined and cannot give an account of them.

"Think about it: when someone doesn't know his own first principle, and both his conclusion and every intermediate step are built on what he cannot justify -- how can such a structure of convention ever qualify as genuine science?"
elegant: No method other than dialectic systematically grasps true existence. The arts deal with opinions and production. Mathematics dreams about being but cannot awaken because it leaves its hypotheses unexamined. Knowledge built on unjustified foundations is convention, not science.
summary: Socrates argues that only dialectic can systematically comprehend all true existence. The practical arts deal with opinions and production. Even mathematics, which partially grasps being, merely dreams about it -- because it relies on unexamined hypotheses. Knowledge built on unjustified first principles cannot be genuine science.
distilled: Mathematics dreams about being because it rests on unexamined hypotheses; only dialectic, which questions first principles, can achieve waking knowledge.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Dream about being' vs. 'waking reality' = the crucial distinction between dianoia and noesis on the Divided Line -- The critique of mathematics: it assumes its axioms (e.g., the existence of numbers, points, lines) without justifying them -- 'Unable to give an account' (logon didonai) = cannot provide a rational justification of first principles -- This is the core argument for dialectic's supremacy: it alone examines and justifies its own foundations, reaching 'the unhypothetical first principle' -- Cross-ref: Book VI (510b-511d) where this argument about mathematical hypotheses is first presented; the 'unhypothetical first principle' (arche anhypothetos) = the Form of the Good

narrative:
"Impossible," he said.

"Then dialectic, and dialectic alone, goes directly to the first principle and is the only science which does away with hypotheses in order to make her ground secure; the eye of the soul, which is literally buried in an outlandish slough, is by her gentle aid lifted upwards; and she uses as handmaids and helpers in the work of conversion, the sciences which we have been discussing. Custom terms them sciences, but they ought to have some other name, implying greater clearness than opinion and less clearness than science: and this, in our previous sketch, was called understanding. But why should we dispute about names when we have realities of such importance to consider?"
refashioned:
"Impossible," Glaucon agreed.

"Then dialectic alone goes directly to the first principle," Socrates said. "It is the only discipline that eliminates hypotheses in order to establish itself on secure ground. The eye of the soul, which is quite literally buried in a barbarous swamp, is gently lifted upward by dialectic. And dialectic uses the mathematical sciences we've discussed as handmaids and helpers in this work of conversion.

"Custom calls them 'sciences,' but they deserve a different name -- something indicating more clarity than opinion but less than true science. In our earlier discussion, we called this level 'understanding.' But why argue about terminology when we have realities of such importance to consider?"
elegant: Dialectic alone reaches the first principle by eliminating hypotheses. It lifts the soul's buried eye upward, using the mathematical sciences as handmaids. These sciences occupy a middle position: clearer than opinion, less clear than dialectical knowledge -- what was earlier called 'understanding.'
summary: Socrates declares that dialectic alone reaches the unhypothetical first principle, eliminating all assumptions. It lifts the soul from its buried state, using mathematics as handmaids. The mathematical disciplines deserve a name between 'opinion' and 'science' -- the 'understanding' (dianoia) of the Divided Line.
distilled: Dialectic alone reaches the first principle without hypotheses; the mathematical sciences serve as its handmaids, occupying a middle rank between opinion and true knowledge.
notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Does away with hypotheses' (tas hypotheseis anairousa) = dialectic's defining method: it examines and transcends the assumptions that mathematics takes for granted -- 'Eye of the soul buried in an outlandish slough' = a powerful image of the soul's degraded condition before philosophical education -- 'Handmaids' (proschronomene) = the mathematical sciences serve dialectic, not the reverse -- The four-part epistemological hierarchy: eikasia (imagination) < pistis (belief) < dianoia (understanding/mathematics) < noesis (dialectical knowledge/science) -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); the 'unhypothetical first principle' (511b)

narrative:
"Why indeed," he said, "when any name will do which expresses the thought of the mind with clearness?"

"At any rate, we are satisfied, as before, to have four divisions; two for intellect and two for opinion, and to call the first division science, the second understanding, the third belief, and the fourth perception of shadows, opinion being concerned with becoming, and intellect with being; and so to make a proportion: --

As being is to becoming, so is pure intellect to opinion.

And as intellect is to opinion, so is science to belief, and understanding to the perception of shadows. But let us defer the further correlation and subdivision of the subjects of opinion and of intellect, for it will be a long enquiry, many times longer than this has been."

"As far as I understand," he said, "I agree."

"And do you also agree, I said, in describing the dialectician as one who attains a conception of the essence of each thing? And he who does not possess and is therefore unable to impart this conception, in whatever degree he fails, may in that degree also be said to fail in intelligence? Will you admit so much?"

"Yes," he said; "how can I deny it?"

"And you would say the same of the conception of the good?"
refashioned:
"Why argue about names," Glaucon said, "when any name that clearly expresses the thought will do?"
